title: Elderberry
subtitle: Sambucus nigra, the hedgerow's autumn berry
excerpt: Single-herb profile. The black-purple berries of the European elder, used in syrups and decoctions through the cold season. Always cooked, never raw.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), elder entry, comprehensive treatment of every part of the tree, including the cyanogenic-glycoside warning on raw berries. Nicholas Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), the seventeenth-century English record. EMA HMPC monograph on Sambuci fructus (2014), modern conservative summary and dosing. Cochrane and PubMed open-access reviews of elderberry-syrup clinical trials (2019, 2021), the evidence base for the cold-season use, conservatively presented.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that traditionally encourages sweating, often used to support the body through a feverish cold.
The deep-purple pigments in elderberries, blackcurrants, blueberries. Water-soluble, heat-sensitive, the constituent set most often associated with the immune-modulating action.

Plant compounds that release small amounts of cyanide when broken down. Present in raw elderberries (and apple seeds, raw bitter almonds). Destroyed by cooking, which is why elderberries are always cooked before eating.

content.text: Elderberry, Sambucus nigra, is the hedgerow's autumn berry, taken in syrups and warm decoctions through the cold season. The deep-purple berries appear in flat-topped clusters in late August through September across the British Isles and continental Europe, on the same tree whose creamy May flowers (elderflower) are the spring cordial. The berries are the herbal medicine kitchen's cold-and-flu staple, recorded in Maud Grieve's Modern Herbal as "the medicine chest of the country people".

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Always cook before eating. Raw elderberries contain cyanogenic glycosides that release small amounts of cyanide when broken down. Cooking destroys these, every traditional preparation simmers the berries for at least 30 minutes, and the publication does not include a single raw-elderberry preparation.

Identification responsibility: if you forage rather than buy dried berries, positive plant ID is your responsibility. Common elder (Sambucus nigra) has black-purple berries in flat-topped clusters. Dwarf elder (Sambucus ebulus) has similar-looking berries but is toxic in adult internal use, and the berries grow upright rather than hanging from a small tree. Mountain ash, deadly nightshade, and pokeweed have all been confused with elderberry by beginning foragers. If you can't positively identify the plant, use cultivated dried berries from a reputable supplier.

Autoimmune conditions: elderberry has immune-modulating activity. Traditional herbal practice advises caution with continuous use in readers with autoimmune conditions (lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, MS, autoimmune thyroid). Discuss with a herbalist before regular use.

Diabetes / cardiac: long-term high-dose elderberry preparations have a mild hypoglycaemic and mild diuretic effect; readers on diabetes medication, blood-pressure medication, or diuretics should mention elderberry-syrup use to their prescriber if taking daily for more than a couple of weeks.

Pregnancy: the cooked-berry syrup at standard cold-season doses (1-2 teaspoons up to three times a day) is widely tolerated. Therapeutic-dose preparations are best discussed with a herbalist or midwife rather than self-prescribed.

content.text: Botanical

Sambucus nigra is a small tree or large shrub of the Adoxaceae family, common in hedgerows, woodland edges, and waste ground across temperate Europe. It reaches 3-5 metres at maturity, with pinnate leaves of 5-7 serrated leaflets and a deeply furrowed grey bark on older specimens.

Flowering: May to early June in southern Britain, late May to mid-June further north. The creamy white flowers grow in flat-topped umbel clusters 10-25 cm across, with a heady fragrance the kitchen meets as elderflower cordial, that flower is a separate materia-medica entry in this library, used quite differently from the berry.

Fruiting: August to September in southern Britain, September to early October further north. The berries appear in the same flat-topped cluster pattern, ripening from green to red to deep purple-black. Ripe clusters hang heavy with the weight; unripe red-stage berries should be left to mature. Birds are fast competitors, clusters left on the tree past the deep-purple stage are usually stripped within a day.

Look-alikes worth knowing: the toxic Sambucus ebulus (dwarf elder) has similar-looking berries but is a herbaceous perennial that dies back each winter rather than a tree, and its berries grow upright on the stem rather than hanging in pendulous clusters. Mountain ash (Sorbus aucuparia) has bright-red berries in similar clusters, colour alone separates them. Deadly nightshade (Atropa belladonna) has solitary black berries on a low herbaceous plant; the cluster pattern is the easy separator.
Part used in this profile: the ripe berries, picked when fully deep-purple. Stems and underripe berries are stripped out at processing, both contain higher concentrations of
cyanogenic glycosides
than the ripe fruit. Dried elderberries from a herbalist supplier are the kitchen norm where foraging isn't practical.
Constituents and actions
The principal constituents of the ripe berry are the
anthocyanins

(cyanidin-3-glucoside and cyanidin-3-sambubioside, the deep-purple pigments), flavonoids (quercetin, rutin), small amounts of phenolic acids, and Vitamin C. The constituents that the modern open-access research most often associates with the immune-modulating action are the anthocyanins; the EMA monograph and the WHO assessment both cite this constituent set as the basis for the syrup's traditional use.
Western herbal practice names three primary actions for the cooked berry:

Immune-modulating action, traditionally the basis for the cold-season syrup. Phrasing matters: the publication's voice describes elderberry as "traditionally taken at the first sign of a cold" rather than "immune-boosting". The Cochrane open-access review (2019) and the case-based clinical trials catalogued through 2021 support the traditional use with limited evidence; the publication describes the evidence at the level it actually warrants.
Diaphoretic

, the warm syrup taken hot through a feverish cold, traditionally credited with supporting the body's own sweating response. Grieve and Culpeper both record this use; modern herbal practice continues it for the supportive feel of a hot sweet syrup rather than for a clinical mechanism.

Mild antiviral, the constituent picture is studied in the open-access literature but the publication holds the verb "mild antiviral" inside the traditional frame rather than claiming a clinical effect. The accurate phrasing is "traditionally taken to support the body through a cold". A reader who wants the syrup as a clinical antiviral wants more evidence than the herbal medicine library can give them, the publication doesn't make the claim.
Traditional uses

Western herbal: Grieve devotes one of the longest entries in the Modern Herbal to the elder, treating bark, leaf, flower, and berry as four separate medicines. The berry, she writes, "makes a cooling drink in feverish complaints, the wine being especially valuable in colds and rheumatism". The hot syrup through a cold was the standard country-medicine use across rural Britain through the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.

Folk European: continental European tradition uses elderberry in winter syrups and Holundersaft (German elder cordial) as a household staple, with the syrup pressed each autumn and stored through the cold season. The Holunderbeerensuppe of Denmark, elderberry soup, is the same tradition in a soup form, served hot with bread dumplings through colds.

Eclectic-American: Felter and Lloyd's King's American Dispensatory (1898) records elderberry as "a popular domestic remedy in colds" and notes its use as a syrup with sugar, taken hot at the first sign of a cold. The Eclectic use mirrored the western European tradition without significant difference.
Preparations

Syrup is the kitchen default. Dried elderberries simmered in water (1:10 by weight, 30 minutes covered, strained), then sweetened 1:1 by volume with honey or sugar. The honey version is the traditional household form, gentler on the throat, longer shelf life refrigerated. Dose: 1-2 teaspoons taken at the first sign of a cold, repeated 3-4 times a day for the duration of an acute episode. Course length: 5-7 days at full dose; longer should be a herbalist's call.

Decoction: dried berries simmered in water as above, strained, drunk warm without the syrup-stage sweetening. Useful in households where added sugar isn't wanted. Same dose pattern as the syrup, in cups rather than spoonfuls. Doesn't keep, make the day's batch fresh.

Tincture: dried berries macerated 1:5 with 40% spirit for 4-6 weeks. Dose 1-3 ml three times a day at the first sign of a cold; not the publication's first recommendation for elderberry, the syrup or decoction is the form the tradition uses and the form the evidence base sits with.

What this profile does not include: raw-berry preparations. There is no traditional or modern western herbal preparation of raw uncooked elderberries; eating them raw, even a handful, causes nausea, vomiting, and stomach cramping. The kitchen's elderberry is always cooked, always strained, always free of stems and underripe red berries.
Cautions and contraindications

Cooked before eating: the rule is absolute. Every traditional preparation simmers the berries for at least 30 minutes; the cooking destroys the cyanogenic glycosides that produce the raw-berry symptoms. The publication's preparations all honour this; the home cook should not modify a recipe to skip the simmer.

Identification responsibility: foraging readers should positively ID the plant. The cluster pattern, the tree-form habit (rather than herbaceous perennial), and the pinnate leaves with 5-7 serrated leaflets are the three confirming features. When in doubt, use cultivated dried berries from a herbalist supplier.

Autoimmune conditions: elderberry has documented immune-modulating activity. Traditional herbal practice advises caution with continuous use in readers with autoimmune conditions, discuss with a herbalist before regular use. Acute short-course use (a 5-7 day syrup course through a cold) is widely tolerated; daily ongoing use as a tonic is the case where a conversation with a herbalist or your GP is the right call.

Drug interactions: no clinically significant interactions documented at standard doses. Long-term high-dose syrups may mildly potentiate diabetes medication (the syrup itself carries sugar/honey, which can affect blood sugar, diabetic readers should account for that), and mild diuretic effect could combine with prescription diuretics. Mention elderberry-syrup use to your prescriber if taking daily for more than a couple of weeks.
Pregnancy: cooked syrup at standard cold-season doses is widely tolerated. Therapeutic-dose tincture preparations are best discussed with a herbalist or midwife.

Children: standard-strength syrup (1-2 teaspoons up to three times a day) is widely tolerated from age 1 upward, traditionally one of the gentler herbal preparations for school-age cold support. Honey-sweetened syrup must not be given to infants under 12 months owing to the rare botulism risk that applies to all raw honey, not just elderberry. For infants, use the sugar-sweetened version at half-strength only with a paediatric herbalist or GP's confirmation.

When to skip and see a doctor: any cold lasting more than ten days, fever rising above 39°C, laboured breathing, or symptoms that worsen after initially improving warrant a GP conversation rather than continued syrup. For children, breathlessness, drowsiness, fluid intake drops, or fever above 39°C in any under-five all need same-day GP review.
